name: Roman Numerals — Letters That Count
whatIsIt: Long before our digits, Romans counted with LETTERS: I=1, V=5, X=10, L=50, C=100. Reading rule: values ADD when they go big→small (VI = 5+1 = 6), but a smaller letter BEFORE a bigger one SUBTRACTS (IV = 5−1 = 4). Only I, X, C can be subtractors, and never more than three of a letter in a row!
whyNeeded: Roman numerals still live all around you: clock faces (III o'clock!), book chapters, movie sequels, class names (Class VI!), monument dates. Reading them is like knowing a secret ancient code that never quite retired.
realLifeUses: Wall clocks with XII at the top
School classes: Class IV, Class VI
Book chapters and movie sequels (Part II!)
Kings and queens in history: Ashoka's edicts, George V

vocabulary.meaning: Small BEFORE big subtracts: IX = 10−1 = 9. Only I, X, C may do this!

story.title: The Old Clock Tower's Letters
story.text: The town's old clock tower shows its numbers in strange letters. 'IV and VI use the same letters — are they the same?' asks Meena. 'Position is everything,' says Thatha. 'IV is 4 — a small letter BEFORE a big one SUBTRACTS: 5 − 1. But VI is 6 — big THEN small ADDS: 5 + 1. Always watch which letter comes first!'
story.extractedProblem: Decode IV and VI — why are they different?
story.answerInStory: IV = 4 (small-before-big subtracts: 5−1); VI = 6 (big-then-small adds: 5+1). Position is everything!

visual.caption: Ancient clock marks: IV = 5−1 = 4 and IX = 10−1 = 9 — small letters BEFORE big ones subtract!

teachingGallery.title: Roman numerals — letters that count
teachingGallery.note: I=1, V=5, X=10. Big-before-small ADD; small-before-big SUBTRACT.

examples.caption: I=1, II=2, III=3 — add the ones.

standardMethod.summary: Scan left to right: add big→small; subtract when a small letter sits before a bigger one.
steps: Know the letters: I=1, V=5, X=10, L=50, C=100.
Going DOWN or level? ADD: XVII = 10+5+1+1 = 17.
Small before big? SUBTRACT that pair: XIV = 10 + (5−1) = 14.
Guard rules: max three repeats (III ✓, IIII ✗); only I, X, C subtract (IV, IX, XL, XC ✓).

alternateMethods.name: Build-by-blocks (writing numbers)
steps: Break the number into tens and ones: 24 = 20 + 4.

alternateMethods.name: Clock-face anchors
steps: Picture the ancient clock: I II III IV V VI VII VIII IX X XI XII.
Anchor the two tricky ones: IV (4) and IX (9) — the subtractors!
Scan: X(10) X(10) V(5) I(1) I(1) — always big→small here.

workedExamples.problem: Ravi writes 40 as XXXX. The tower rumbles! Fix his code.
steps: Guard rule: never FOUR repeats of a letter.
40 needs the subtractor trick with L(50): small X before big L.

trickPractice.intro: Smaller letter before a bigger one? Subtract. Otherwise just add them up.

commonMistakes.mistake: Reading IV and VI as the same (both '1 and 5 = 6').
commonMistakes.fix: Position is the whole code! Small BEFORE big subtracts (IV = 4); big before small adds (VI = 6). Scan left to right and watch for the small-first pairs.

commonMistakes.mistake: Writing 4 as IIII or 40 as XXXX.
commonMistakes.fix: Three's the max! The fourth step always uses a subtractor: 4 = IV, 9 = IX, 40 = XL, 90 = XC.

commonMistakes.mistake: Inventing subtractions like VL for 45 or VX for 5.
commonMistakes.fix: Only I, X, and C may stand as subtractors — V and L never do. 45 = XL + V = XLV.

hintLadder: X then V — big to small.

options.label: 9 — small I before big X subtracts

easy.answer: 9 — small I before big X subtracts
hintLadder: Is the I before or after the X?

medium.q: The correct Roman code for 4 is…
hintLadder: The fourth step uses a subtractor.

challenge.q: Ravi writes 45 as VL ('50 minus 5!'). The tower rumbles again. Why?

options.label: V is never a subtractor — 45 = XL + V = XLV

challenge.answer: V is never a subtractor — 45 = XL + V = XLV

questions.q: Which letters are ALLOWED to subtract?
hintLadder: IV, IX, XL, XC — spot the pattern!

teachBackPrompt: Become the tower keeper! Teach someone at home the two rules (add big→small, subtract small-before-big) using a real or drawn clock face — and catch them if they ever write IIII!

revisionCard.summary: I=1, V=5, X=10, L=50, C=100. Big→small ADDS (XVII = 17); small-before-big SUBTRACTS (IV = 4, IX = 9, XL = 40). Guards: max 3 repeats; only I, X, C subtract. Build by blocks: 39 = XXX + IX = XXXIX.

gameMission.title: The Ancient Clock Tower Code
gameMission.brief: The tower's letter-locks are scrambled! Decode Roman marks to open each floor, re-encode our numbers into proper Roman (no IIII allowed!), and silence the rumbles from Ravi's forbidden VLs.

realLifeProject: Roman hunt: find 3 Roman numerals in the wild (a clock, a book's chapter list, a movie title, a class board). Decode each aloud — then write your own age and birth month in Roman!

rememberIt.unpack: 'I Value Xylophones Like Cows Do Milk'. Smaller BEFORE bigger = subtract (IV=4, IX=9). Smaller after = add (VI=6). Never four in a row.

studentQuestions.q: Why is 4 written IV, not IIII?
studentQuestions.a: A smaller numeral BEFORE a bigger one means subtract: IV = 5-1 = 4. It avoids four in a row.

studentQuestions.q: How do I read a mix like XIV?
studentQuestions.a: Left to right, adding, but subtract when a smaller symbol sits before a bigger one. X + IV = 10 + 4 = 14.
